structuredFacts.title: Apple Inc.
structuredFacts.subtitle: American multinational technology company
structuredFacts.leadParagraph:
Apple Inc. is an American multinational technology company headquartered in Cupertino, California, in Silicon Valley, and known for consumer electronics, software and online services. Founded in 1976 as Apple Computer Company by Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak and Ronald Wayne, the company was incorporated by Jobs and Wozniak as Apple Computer, Inc. the following year. Its current name was adopted in 2007 as the company expanded its focus from computers to consumer electronics. Apple is one of the Big Tech companies.
The company was founded to market Wozniak's Apple I computer. Its successor, the Apple II, became one of the first successful mass-produced microcomputers. Apple introduced the Lisa in 1983 and the Macintosh in 1984, popularizing graphical user interfaces navigated by a mouse. By 1985, internal conflicts led to Jobs leaving the company to form NeXT and Wozniak stepping back from active employment. During the 1990s, Apple lost considerable market share to lower-priced Wintel computers, Intel-powered PC clones running Windows. By 1997, Apple was reporting major losses and struggling to deliver a modernized operating system, leading Apple to acquire NeXT, which brought Jobs back to the company. Under his leadership, Apple returned to profitability through the iMac, iPod, iPhone, and iPad, the iTunes Store, the Apple Store retail chain, and the "Think different" advertising campaign. Jobs resigned in 2011 for health reasons and died later that year. He was succeeded as CEO by Tim Cook, who will be succeeded by John Ternus in September 2026.
Apple's product lineup includes portable and home hardware like the iPhone, iPad, Mac, Apple Watch, and AirPods; several in-house operating systems such as iOS, iPadOS, and macOS; and various software and services including Apple Pay and iCloud, as well as multimedia streaming services like Apple Music and Apple TV. Since 2011, Apple has for the most part been the world's largest company by market capitalization, and, as of 2024, is the largest manufacturing company by revenue, the fourth-largest PC vendor, the largest vendor of tablet computers, and the largest vendor of mobile phones. Apple became the first publicly traded US company to be valued at over $1 trillion in 2018, and, as of October 2025, is valued at just over $4 trillion.
Apple has received criticism regarding its contractors' labor conditions, its relationship with trade unions, its environmental practices, and its corporate ethics, including anti-competitive tactics, materials sourcing, and its acquisitions of smaller businesses. Nevertheless, the company has a large following and enjoys a high level of customer loyalty. Apple has consistently been ranked as one of the world's most valuable brands since the late 2000s.
structuredFacts.briefSummary: Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne founded Apple in 1976 to market the Apple I motherboard. To fund this first run of production, Jobs sold his Volkswagen Bus while Wozniak parted with his HP-65 calculator. Only twelve days into the venture, Wayne sold his entire ten percent stake back to his co-founders for $800. This modest start gave way to rapid growth, with annual sales surging from $775,000 in September 1977 to $118 million by September 1980. Following decades of expansion, the company officially dropped "Computer" from its name in 2007. Today, Tim Cook leads the company as CEO, guiding Apple to a market capitalization that surpassed $4 trillion in October 2025.

cards.title: Funding the First Apple
cards.summary: When Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne founded Apple in 1976, their immediate goal was marketing the Apple I motherboard. To fund the production of this hand-built circuit board, the young partners desperately needed upfront capital. Jobs sold his Volkswagen Bus and Wozniak parted with his HP-65 calculator to raise the necessary cash. While this sacrifice secured their initial funding, Wayne chose to minimize his financial risk through a swift exit. Just twelve days after helping launch the company, Wayne sold his ten percent share back for eight hundred dollars. Ultimately, a mix of extreme personal sacrifice and an early partner departure set Apple in motion.
cards.readerQuestion: What personal sacrifices were required to fund the very first Apple computer?
cards.keyTakeaway: Extreme personal sacrifices and early partner departures launched Apple.

cards.title: The Devilish Price of Innovation
cards.summary: Debuting in 1976, the hand-built Apple I motherboard carried a highly unusual, devilish retail price of $666.66. Its successor, the Apple II, achieved major success when paired with the VisiCalc spreadsheet program. This killer software boosted the machine's popularity, leading to a massive IPO in 1980. By raising over $100 million and creating hundreds of millionaires, Apple transformed from a garage startup into a public phenomenon. The company's annual sales skyrocketed from $775,000 in September 1977 to $118 million by September 1980. Ultimately, this mix of quirky pricing and powerful software sparked a historic financial revolution.
cards.readerQuestion: How did a uniquely priced motherboard and a spreadsheet program spark a financial revolution?
cards.keyTakeaway: Unusual pricing and killer software sparked a historic financial revolution.

cards.title: The Spark of the Mac
cards.summary: Steve Jobs secured rare access to Xerox PARC's graphical user interface technology in 1979. To negotiate this exclusive preview, Jobs offered Xerox $1 million in pre-IPO Apple stock. This technology directly inspired the development of Apple's GUI-based Lisa and Macintosh computers. Building on these visual interfaces, Apple launched the Macintosh with a cinematic Super Bowl commercial. Director Ridley Scott filmed the landmark '1984' advertisement, which cost $1.5 million to air. Ultimately, Xerox's interface ideas fueled Apple's revolutionary Macintosh launch.
cards.readerQuestion: How did a visit to Xerox PARC shape the future of personal computing interfaces?
cards.keyTakeaway: Xerox's interface ideas fueled Apple's revolutionary Macintosh launch.

cards.title: The Evolution Beyond Silicon
cards.summary: Apple launched the Macintosh in 1984 with a famous Ridley Scott commercial, but slow sales quickly triggered executive friction. This sluggish market performance created intense internal tension over the company's future direction. Ultimately, the resulting corporate power struggle led to the departure of co-founder Steve Jobs. Following this leadership dispute, Apple began a long-term shift toward a broader consumer electronics identity. To reflect this new era, the company officially dropped 'Computer' from its name in 2007. Internal rivalry and strategic rebranding had transformed Apple from a niche computer maker into a global tech giant.
cards.readerQuestion: What internal conflicts and strategic pivots redefined Apple's identity beyond computers?
cards.keyTakeaway: Internal rivalry and rebranding transformed Apple into a tech giant.

cards.title: The Four Trillion Dollar Era
cards.summary: Tim Cook assumed leadership of Apple as CEO in 2011 . He immediately implemented scaling strategies that drove unprecedented financial growth . The tech giant crossed the historic one trillion dollar market capitalization milestone in 2018 . This massive momentum ultimately secured a four trillion dollar valuation and a structured 2026 transition plan . By October 2025, Apple's market capitalization officially surpassed just over four trillion dollars . Cook's financial triumph secures a stable 2026 leadership transition .
cards.readerQuestion: How did Tim Cook's era pave the way for Apple's next generation of leadership?
cards.keyTakeaway: Cook's financial triumph secures a stable 2026 leadership transition.

timeline.headline: Apple Computer Founded

timeline.description: Apple incorporates in Cupertino and introduces the Apple II, establishing the home computer market.

timeline.description: Apple goes public on Nasdaq, raising over $100 million and creating more than 300 millionaires.
